Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract AbstractTransition metal oxides often undergo dynamic surface reconstruction to an active state under oxygen evolution reaction (OER) conditions, which is pH-dependent. The pH dependency of the OER catalytic activity is commonly reported but not yet fully understood. In this study, the Co oxidation state at pH-dependent (hydr)oxide/electrolyte interfaces was probed via operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy. Based on the combination of in situ electrochemical measurements and X-ray absorption spectroscopy, the Co redox dynamics and the flat band potential were correlated with Co oxidation state changes to understand the pH-dependent OER activity. The low flat band potential at the alkaline condition resulted in low Co redox potential under the OER condition, which is favorable for the surface reconstruction. In contrast, the anodic shift of the Co redox potential at neutral and acidic conditions leads to the increase of the OER overpotential. The larger overpotential at neutral condition was ascribed to the lower Co atom polarizability and the slower Co oxidation state changes. The work here reveals that the interfacial Co oxidation state changes are the key factor to determine the pH-dependent OER activity.
